caseStudies.title: Wong's Bakery — April 20X5
caseStudies.text:
Miss Wong started a bakery business in April 20X5. The following transactions occurred during the month:

a. Miss Wong invested $100,000 cash into the business and contributed a convection oven valued at $24,000 (estimated useful life 10 years, zero residual value, straight-line depreciation).
b. Paid $15,000 cash for shop furniture (estimated useful life 5 years, zero residual value, straight-line depreciation).
c. Paid $2,000 cash for business licences (one-off expense).
d. Paid $18,000 cash for 6-month shop rental from 1 April.
e. Purchased $2,000 of baking materials on credit; $300 worth remained unused at month-end.
f. Paid $12,000 cash for a 12-month insurance policy effective 15 April.
g. Settled the baking materials payable in full with cash.
h. Recorded revenue from baking sales during April of $45,000 (assume all received in cash).
i. Paid $8,000 cash for staff salaries for April.
j. Recognised monthly depreciation on all depreciable assets.

caseStudies.title: Green Foods Ltd — Profitability Analysis
caseStudies.text:
The following income statements are provided for Green Foods Ltd:

                        20X4          20X3
Revenue              $2,000,000    $1,800,000
Cost of sales        $1,100,000      $900,000
Gross profit           $900,000      $900,000
Operating expenses     $399,760      $461,865
Profit before tax      $500,240      $438,135
Tax (29%)              $145,000      $119,000
Net profit             $355,240      $319,135

Additional information:
- Total assets 20X4: $3,200,000; 20X3: $2,900,000
- Total assets 20X2: $2,700,000
- Current assets 20X4: $1,100,000; Current liabilities 20X4: $700,000
- Current assets 20X3: $980,000; Current liabilities 20X3: $620,000
- Industry average current ratio: 1.8×
- Industry average gross margin: 48%
- Industry average net profit margin: 15%

caseStudies.title: CVP Analysis — Sunshine Accessories
caseStudies.text:
Sunshine Accessories manufactures a single product, a fashion bag, and sells it for $120 each. The variable cost is $72 per unit and total fixed costs are $216,000 per year.

Additional data:
- Last year actual sales: 6,000 units
- Tax rate: 20%
- The company is considering reducing the selling price to $110 to increase market share

caseStudies.title: Budget and Variance Analysis — Coastal Beverages
caseStudies.text:
Coastal Beverages Ltd prepared the following static budget for the quarter:
- Budgeted sales: 20,000 units at $15 per unit
- Budgeted variable costs: $8 per unit
- Budgeted fixed costs: $70,000

Actual results for the quarter:
- Units sold: 22,000
- Actual revenue: $319,000
- Actual variable costs: $182,600
- Actual fixed costs: $73,000

questions.label: Question 1a
questions.text: Prepare journal entries for ALL transactions (a) to (j) for Wong's Bakery in April 20X5. Show debit and credit entries clearly with narrations.
questions.modelAnswer:
(a) Capital investment — cash and oven:
DR Cash                      $100,000
DR Convection Oven (Asset)    $24,000
   CR Capital (Owner's Equity)        $124,000
(Being Miss Wong's initial investment of cash and oven)

(b) Purchase of shop furniture:
DR Shop Furniture             $15,000
   CR Cash                             $15,000
(Being purchase of shop furniture for cash)

(c) Business licences:
DR Licence Expense             $2,000
   CR Cash                              $2,000
(Being payment for business licences, expensed immediately as one-off)

(d) Prepaid rent:
DR Prepaid Rent               $18,000
   CR Cash                             $18,000
(Being 6-month rent paid in advance from 1 April)

Adjusting entry — 1 month rent elapsed = $18,000 / 6 = $3,000:
DR Rent Expense                $3,000
   CR Prepaid Rent                      $3,000
(Being rent expense recognised for April)

(e) Purchase of baking materials on credit:
DR Baking Materials (Asset)    $2,000
   CR Accounts Payable                  $2,000
(Being purchase of baking materials on credit)

Adjusting entry — materials used = $2,000 – $300 = $1,700:
DR Baking Materials Expense    $1,700
   CR Baking Materials (Asset)          $1,700
(Being cost of baking materials consumed during April)

(f) Prepaid insurance:
DR Prepaid Insurance          $12,000
   CR Cash                             $12,000
(Being 12-month insurance paid in advance from 15 April)

Adjusting entry — approximately 0.5 month elapsed (15 Apr to 30 Apr):
Insurance expense = $12,000 / 12 × 0.5 = $500
DR Insurance Expense             $500
   CR Prepaid Insurance                  $500
(Being insurance expense for half of April recognised)

(g) Settlement of accounts payable:
DR Accounts Payable            $2,000
   CR Cash                              $2,000
(Being settlement of baking materials payable)

(h) Revenue from baking sales:
DR Cash                       $45,000
   CR Baking Sales Revenue             $45,000
(Being cash sales revenue for April)

(i) Salaries paid:
DR Salaries Expense            $8,000
   CR Cash                              $8,000
(Being salaries paid to staff for April)

(j) Monthly depreciation:
Convection Oven: $24,000 / 10 years / 12 months = $200/month
Shop Furniture: $15,000 / 5 years / 12 months = $250/month
Total depreciation = $200 + $250 = $450

DR Depreciation Expense          $450
   CR Accumulated Depreciation — Oven    $200
   CR Accumulated Depreciation — Furniture $250
(Being monthly depreciation on convection oven and shop furniture)
keyPoints: Transaction (a): both Cash AND the oven are debited as assets; Capital is credited
Prepaid rent and prepaid insurance are initially recorded as assets, then adjusted
Baking materials: record as asset first, then expense the portion used ($1,700)
Insurance: only ~0.5 month elapsed (15 Apr to 30 Apr), so $500 is expensed
Depreciation is calculated separately for each asset and credited to accumulated depreciation
Licence fee is immediately expensed (no future economic benefit beyond current period)

questions.label: Question 1b
questions.text:
Based on the journal entries prepared, calculate:
(i) Profit for the month of April 20X5
(ii) Balance of Owner's Equity as at 30 April 20X5

subItems.label: (ii)
subItems.text: Balance of Owner's Equity as at 30 April 20X5

questions.modelAnswer:
(i) Profit for April 20X5

Revenue:
Baking Sales                  $45,000

Less Expenses:
Licence Expense                $2,000
Rent Expense                   $3,000   (18,000 / 6 months)
Baking Materials Expense       $1,700   (2,000 – 300 unused)
Insurance Expense                $500   (12,000 / 12 × 0.5 months)
Salaries Expense               $8,000
Depreciation — Oven              $200   (24,000 / 120 months)
Depreciation — Furniture         $250   (15,000 / 60 months)
Total Expenses                $15,650

Profit for April = $45,000 – $15,650 = $29,350

(ii) Owner's Equity as at 30 April 20X5

Opening Capital (Investment)  $124,000   (Cash $100,000 + Oven $24,000)
Add: Profit for April          $29,350
Less: Drawings                     $0   (none mentioned)
Closing Owner's Equity        $153,350
keyPoints: Only include expenses that were INCURRED in April (not prepaid amounts)
Rent: 1 month out of 6 = $3,000 (not the full $18,000)
Insurance: half month only = $500 (policy started 15 April)
Baking materials: only the used portion ($1,700), not total purchased
Capital includes both cash ($100,000) AND the oven at fair value ($24,000)
Owner's equity = Opening capital + Profit – Drawings

questions.label: Question 1c
questions.text: Calculate the asset turnover ratio for Wong's Bakery for April 20X5. Comment on the ratio compared to a benchmark of 1.0 (annualised). Assume total assets as at 30 April 20X5.
questions.modelAnswer:
Asset Turnover Ratio = Revenue / Total Assets

First, calculate Total Assets as at 30 April 20X5:
Cash = 100,000 – 15,000 – 2,000 – 18,000 – 12,000 – 2,000 + 45,000 – 8,000 = $88,000
Convection Oven (net) = 24,000 – 200 = $23,800
Shop Furniture (net) = 15,000 – 250 = $14,750
Prepaid Rent = 18,000 – 3,000 = $15,000
Prepaid Insurance = 12,000 – 500 = $11,500
Baking Materials = $300 (unused)
Total Assets = 88,000 + 23,800 + 14,750 + 15,000 + 11,500 + 300 = $153,350

Note: Total Assets = Owner's Equity (no liabilities remaining as payable was settled) = $153,350 ✓

Monthly Asset Turnover = $45,000 / $153,350 = 0.293×
Annualised = 0.293 × 12 = 3.52×

Comment: The annualised asset turnover ratio of 3.52× significantly exceeds the benchmark of 1.0×, indicating that Wong's Bakery is generating $3.52 in revenue for every $1 of assets. This is a very strong result, suggesting highly efficient use of assets. However, as this is only the first month of operations, the ratio should be monitored over a longer period before drawing firm conclusions.
keyPoints: Asset turnover = Revenue / Total Assets
Annualise the ratio by multiplying monthly figure by 12 for fair comparison to annual benchmark
Total assets should match owner's equity if there are no remaining liabilities
Comment must address whether ratio is above/below benchmark and what it implies
Acknowledge the limitation: only one month of data for a new business

questions.label: Question 2a
questions.text:
For Green Foods Ltd, calculate the following ratios for both 20X4 and 20X3:
(i) Gross margin
(ii) Net profit margin
(iii) Return on total assets
(iv) Sales growth (20X4 vs 20X3)
(v) Profit growth (20X4 vs 20X3)

subItems.label: (iii)
subItems.text: Return on total assets for 20X4 and 20X3

keyPoints: Gross margin uses GROSS profit over revenue
Net profit margin uses NET profit (after tax) over revenue
ROA uses AVERAGE total assets — average of opening and closing balances
Growth rates: (Current – Prior) / Prior × 100
Profit grew slightly faster (11.31%) than sales (11.11%) — positive sign

questions.label: Question 2b
questions.text: Analyse the profitability trends of Green Foods Ltd based on the ratios calculated in Q2a. Compare each ratio to the industry averages provided and identify key strengths and concerns.
questions.modelAnswer:
Analysis of Green Foods Ltd Profitability Trends:

GROSS MARGIN:
The gross margin declined from 50.0% (20X3) to 45.0% (20X4), a fall of 5 percentage points. Both years are below the industry average of 48%, with 20X4 declining further. This signals that cost of sales has risen faster than revenue — possibly due to higher input/material costs, or the company reducing prices to achieve sales growth. Management should investigate supply chain costs and pricing strategy.

NET PROFIT MARGIN:
Despite the gross margin decline, the net profit margin remained stable at approximately 17.76% (20X4) vs 17.73% (20X3). Both exceed the industry average of 15%, which is a strength. The stability of net margin despite lower gross margin suggests that operating expenses were successfully reduced (from $461,865 to $399,760), partially offsetting the gross margin erosion.

RETURN ON TOTAL ASSETS:
ROA improved marginally from 11.40% to 11.65%, showing slightly better asset utilisation. Both years are performing reasonably given strong profit growth.

OVERALL:
Strengths: Strong net profit margins exceeding industry; healthy sales and profit growth of ~11%.
Concerns: Declining gross margin — if this trend continues, it will eventually erode net profitability. The company must address cost of sales or justify why it is pricing below industry norms.
keyPoints: Reference specific ratio values and compare to industry averages
Identify the key tension: gross margin declining but net margin stable (due to opex reduction)
Strong points: net margin above industry, consistent growth
Concern: gross margin below industry average and declining
Provide actionable insight, not just description of numbers

questions.label: Question 2c
questions.text: Calculate the current ratio for Green Foods Ltd for 20X4 and 20X3. Compare the results to the industry average of 1.8× and comment on the company's liquidity position.
questions.modelAnswer:
Current Ratio = Current Assets / Current Liabilities

20X4: $1,100,000 / $700,000 = 1.57×
20X3: $980,000 / $620,000 = 1.58×
Industry average: 1.8×

Analysis:
The current ratio has remained almost unchanged at approximately 1.57–1.58× in both years. Both results are below the industry average of 1.8×, indicating that Green Foods Ltd has a weaker liquidity position relative to its peers.

While the company can still cover its current liabilities (ratio > 1.0), the gap versus industry suggests:
- Less buffer if unexpected cash demands arise
- Potential reliance on short-term credit or tight payment cycles

However, the stability of the ratio indicates the liquidity position is not deteriorating further. Management should monitor working capital closely and consider strategies to improve the ratio, such as extending payment terms with suppliers, speeding up receivables collection, or reducing short-term debt.
keyPoints: Current ratio = Current Assets / Current Liabilities
Both years are below industry average of 1.8× — unfavourable
Ratio is stable — not worsening, but not improving
A ratio above 1.0 means current assets exceed current liabilities (can cover short-term obligations)
Suggest practical improvement actions

questions.label: Question 3a
questions.text:
For Sunshine Accessories, calculate:
(i) Contribution margin per unit and contribution margin ratio
(ii) Break-even point in units and in sales dollars
(iii) Profit at last year's actual sales of 6,000 units
(iv) Profit after tax at 6,000 units

subItems.label: (i)
subItems.text: Contribution margin per unit and CM ratio

subItems.label: (ii)
subItems.text: Break-even point in units and dollars

questions.modelAnswer:
(i) Contribution Margin
CM per unit = Selling Price – Variable Cost = $120 – $72 = $48
CM Ratio = $48 / $120 × 100 = 40%

(ii) Break-Even Point
Break-Even Units = Fixed Costs / CM per unit = $216,000 / $48 = 4,500 units
Break-Even Sales = 4,500 × $120 = $540,000
Alternatively: $216,000 / 0.40 = $540,000 ✓

(iii) Profit at 6,000 units
Total Revenue = 6,000 × $120 = $720,000
Total Variable Costs = 6,000 × $72 = $432,000
Contribution Margin = $720,000 – $432,000 = $288,000
Less Fixed Costs = $216,000
Profit before tax = $288,000 – $216,000 = $72,000

Alternatively: (Units – BEP) × CM per unit = (6,000 – 4,500) × $48 = 1,500 × $48 = $72,000 ✓

(iv) Profit After Tax
Tax = $72,000 × 20% = $14,400
Profit after tax = $72,000 – $14,400 = $57,600
keyPoints: CM = Selling price minus variable costs only
Break-even: Fixed costs / CM per unit — only fixed costs, not total costs
At 6,000 units, the company sells 1,500 units above break-even
Profit after tax = Profit before tax × (1 – tax rate)
Always show formula, numbers substituted, and final answer with units/labels

questions.label: Question 3b
questions.text:
Sunshine Accessories is considering reducing the selling price to $110 per unit to increase sales volume.
(i) Calculate the new break-even point in units.
(ii) Calculate the sales volume needed at the new price to achieve the same profit ($72,000) as before.
(iii) Advise management on whether this price reduction is beneficial.

subItems.label: (i)
subItems.text: New break-even point at $110 selling price

subItems.label: (ii)
subItems.text: Units needed to achieve $72,000 profit at new price

questions.modelAnswer:
(i) New Break-Even Point
New CM per unit = $110 – $72 = $38
New Break-Even Units = $216,000 / $38 = 5,684 units (rounded up to 5,685)

Comparison: Break-even increased from 4,500 to 5,685 units — a 26% increase in break-even volume.

(ii) Units to Achieve $72,000 Profit
Required Units = (Fixed Costs + Target Profit) / New CM per unit
= ($216,000 + $72,000) / $38
= $288,000 / $38
= 7,579 units (rounded up)

Comparison: Previously needed 6,000 units; now needs 7,579 units — an additional 1,579 units (26.3% more).

(iii) Management Recommendation
The price reduction is risky. While a lower price may attract more customers, the company would need to increase sales volume by 26% (from 6,000 to 7,579 units) just to maintain the same profit. The break-even point also rises significantly. Management should only proceed if market research confirms that demand at $110 will exceed 7,579 units. If demand elasticity is low (customers are not very price-sensitive), the price cut would reduce profitability without a compensating volume increase.
keyPoints: New CM = new price minus SAME variable cost
Target profit formula: (FC + Target Profit) / CM per unit
Compare old and new break-even volumes to highlight increased risk
Recommendation must be logically justified using the numbers
Key concept: lower price reduces CM, requiring higher volume to compensate

questions.label: Question 4a
questions.text:
For Coastal Beverages Ltd:
(i) Prepare a flexible budget for the actual activity level of 22,000 units.
(ii) Calculate ALL variances (revenue, variable cost, fixed cost, and operating profit) and label each as Favourable (F) or Unfavourable (U).
(iii) Calculate the sales volume variance by comparing flexible budget profit to static budget profit.

subItems.label: (ii)
subItems.text: All variances labelled F or U

questions.modelAnswer:
(i) Flexible Budget at 22,000 Units
                        Flexible Budget (22,000 units)
Revenue (22,000 × $15)        $330,000
Variable Costs (22,000 × $8)  $176,000
Contribution Margin           $154,000
Fixed Costs                    $70,000
Operating Profit               $84,000

(ii) Variance Analysis
                    Flexible Budget   Actual      Variance
Revenue             $330,000          $319,000    $11,000 (U)
Variable Costs      $176,000          $182,600     $6,600 (U)
Contribution Margin $154,000          $136,400    $17,600 (U)
Fixed Costs          $70,000           $73,000     $3,000 (U)
Operating Profit     $84,000           $63,400    $20,600 (U)

Actual selling price = $319,000 / 22,000 = $14.50 vs budget $15.00 → price variance (U)
Actual variable cost per unit = $182,600 / 22,000 = $8.30 vs budget $8.00 → efficiency variance (U)

(iii) Sales Volume Variance
Static Budget Profit:
Revenue (20,000 × $15) = $300,000
Variable Costs (20,000 × $8) = $160,000
Contribution = $140,000
Fixed Costs = $70,000
Static Budget Profit = $70,000

Sales Volume Variance = Flexible Budget Profit – Static Budget Profit
= $84,000 – $70,000 = $14,000 (F)

The higher volume (22,000 vs 20,000 units) generated a favourable volume variance of $14,000. However, the total operating profit variance is $63,400 – $70,000 = $6,600 (U) vs static budget, driven by adverse price and cost variances more than offsetting the volume benefit.
keyPoints: Flexible budget: variable costs scaled to actual volume; fixed costs remain unchanged
Revenue variance: actual revenue vs flexible budget revenue (price effect)
Variable cost variance: actual variable cost vs flexible budget variable cost (efficiency effect)
Sales volume variance = flexible budget profit vs static budget profit
All four variances here are unfavourable except the volume effect
Label every variance as F (favourable) or U (unfavourable) — favourable = better than budget
